content.full_text:
State of California
Copy also filed: 18703

Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 867 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance     Enforcement     Executive/Legal     Enforcement     Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 322 5660          322 5660          122 5901            322 6441           322 6444

April 13, 1981

A-81-04-020

Honorable Byron Sher
California State Assembly
State Capitol, Rm. 2176
Sacramento, CA  95814

Re:  Request for Advice for Robert Reese

Dear Assemblyman Sher:

You have requested written advice regarding your constituent Robert Reese, who is seeking a city council seat. You advise that he is a member of an association which engaged in the practice of real estate appraisal. You advise that another member of the association has the city as a client. You further advise that there is neither fee splitting nor profit sharing among members of the association. By way of telephone communication, your aide, Mark Chandler, advises that the association office is located 10 minutes outside the jurisdiction (city) and that the city in question is metropolitan.

You have posed two specific questions: (1) Whether Mr. Reese would have to disqualify himself with regard to any matters concerning clients of other members of the association. (2) Whether any general conflict of interest considerations arise for Mr. Reese by virtue of the relationship between the city and another member of the association.

Discussion

The Political Reform Act, and specifically Government Code Section 87100, provides that:

     No public official at any level of state or local government shall make, participate in making or in any way attempt to use his official position to influence a governmental decision in which he knows or has reason to know he has a financial interest.

Honorable Byron Sher
April 13, 1981
Page Two


     Government Code Section 87103 defines financial interest
as follows:

          An official has a financial interest in a decision
     within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably
     foreseeable that the decision will have a material
     financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on
     the public generally, on:

          (a)  Any business entity in which the public
     official has a direct or indirect investment worth
     more than one thousand dollars ($1,000).

          (b)  Any real property in which the public
     official has a direct or indirect interest worth
     more than one thousand dollars ($1,000).

          (c)  Any source of income, other than loans
     by a commercial lending institution in the regular
     course of business on terms available to the public
     without regard to official status, aggregating two
     hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more in value
     provided to, received by or promised to the public
     official within 12 months prior to the time when
     the decision is made.

          (d)  Any business entity in which the public
     official is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
     employee, or holds any position of management.
     For purposes of this section, indirect investment
     or interest means any investment or interest owned
     by the spouse or dependent child of a public offi-
     cial, by an agent on behalf of a public official,
     or by a business entity or trust in which the
     official, the official's agents, spouse, and de-
     pendent children own directly, indirectly, or
     beneficially a 10 percent interest or greater.

                    (Emphasis added.)

     The Fair Political Practices Commission has adopted
regulations further defining what constitutes a "material
financial effect" (2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18702) and dis-
tinguishing that from an "effect on the public generally" (2
Cal. Adm. Code Section 18703). The text of those regulations
is appended hereto for your convenience.

Honorable Byron Sher
April 13, 1981
Page Three


Analysis

Mr. Reese need disqualify himself only "if it is reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial
effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally,
on" any source of income to Mr. Reese aggregating $250 or
more in the preceding 12 months.

Material financial effect, as previously noted, is
defined in 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18702. Mr. Reese must
examine the criteria there to determine if any of the tests
are met. Thus, if Mr. Reese's income will increase by more
than the stated amount, as a result of any given decision
which he might make, then he would have to disqualify himself
unless the decision would equally affect the public generally.
(See 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18703.) It is not likely that
real estate appraising is so prevalent a profession in any
city that it would "constitute a significant segment of the
public generally." Consequently, that exception is not
likely to be available.

Therefore, as to any such decision Mr. Reese should
apply the tests of materiality and foreseeability expressed
in the regulations. If the tests are met, then disqualifica-
tion would be required. See the Thorner Opinion, mentioned
below.

Question 1 Disqualification Relative to Matters Affecting
Client of Another Member.

Based upon the facts given, clients of Mr. Reese's
associates are not a source of income to him or to the asso-
ciation itself, since there is no profit-sharing or fee
splitting. He would not have to disqualify himself.

Question 2 General Conflict of Interest Considerations.

Mr. Reese's fellow associate has as a client the City.
This client will be treated the same way as any client under
the analysis of Question 1 above. However, under the Commission's
ruling in the Thorner Opinion, 1 FPPC Opinion 198 (No. 75-
089, 1975), relying, in part, on U.S. v. Mississippi Valley
Generating Co., 364 U.S. 520 (1961), as authority, the Commission
considered the federal statutory basis for the Supreme
Court's decision. The federal statute quoted, read as follows:

"Interested Persons Acting As Government Agents.
Whoever, being an officer, agent or member of, or directly
or indirectly interested in the pecuniary profits or
contracts of any corporation, joint-stock company, or

Honorable Byron Sher
April 13, 1981
Page Four


association, or of any firm or partnership, or other
business entity, is employed or acts as an officer of
the United States for the transaction of business with
such business entity shall be fined..." 18 U.S.C.
Section 201, formerly 18 U.S.C. Section 434. 1 FPPC
198, at 203 fn. 2.

(Emphasis added.)

While 18 U.S.C. Section 201 does not directly apply to local
government officials, we think the spirit embodied there is
applicable here. Consequently, Mr. Reese may wish to consider
disqualifying himself (if elected) from any decision involving
his fellow associate with respect to the City as a client of
his.

Beyond these two issues is the more general one. If a
decision by the City Council will cause a substantial change
in property values which, in turn, would foreseeably create
a greater need for real estate appraisals in the community,
then this could possibly have a material financial effect on
Mr. Reese's income, directly, which need amount to only
$100 1/ since his source of income is his client seeking the
real estate appraisal. This may not be foreseeable, given
that Mr. Reese's business is outside the jurisdiction. It
would depend on whether he gets clients from within the
city. 2/

Conclusion

As to any decisions which Mr. Reese might make, should
he become a member of the City Council, which would foreseeably materially financially affect his income or that of his
association, he will have to disqualify himself. In each
instance, in order to determine that necessary, he will have
to apply the tests described above.


1/ See 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18702(b)(3)(A).

2/ See, again, the Thorner Opinion, copy attached for
your convenience.

Honorable Byron Sher
April 13, 1981
Page Five


     If Mr. Reese is successful in his bid to join the City
Council and wishes the Commission's advice on specific matters,
then he should write and request an advice letter at that
time. Without more specific, detailed information it is not
possible to give more precise, detailed advice. Should you
or Mr. Reese have further questions, please contact me.

                                        Very truly yours,

                                        [signature]
                                        Robert E. Leigh
                                        Counsel
                                        Legal Division

REL:kp

cc: Mark Chandler, Administrative Asst.
    District Office of Assemblyman Byron Sher

Enclosures




P.S. In addition, as I have discussed with Mr. Chandler,
Mr. Reese may wish to seek advice from the
Attorney General with regard to the possible
applicability of Government Code Section
1090, et seq. as those sections relate to the
issues of Question No. 2.

See Final ^correspondence Filed:
18702

See copy filed also in 18703

State of California

Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5602          322-5650          322-5901          322-4441          322-6444

March 11, 1981

Assemblyman Byron Sher
State Assembly
State Capitol
Sacramento, CA  95814

Dear Assemblyman Sher:

     Thank you for your request for advice regarding your
constituent.  However, we cannot give specific advice unless
the identity of the person for whom the advice is sought is
provided.  See 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18329(b).  Nevertheless,
I can give you some general guidelines which may be helpful
to your constituent.

     Disqualification is required under Government Code Section
87100 only in the case of a decision which has a material
financial effect on a business entity or real property in
which the official has an interest, or on a source of income
to the official.  As a general rule, an official is not required
to disqualify himself or herself in matters which affect a
source of income to a business partner or associate, but
which do not directly affect a source of income to an investment
of the official.  Whether a decision has a material financial
effect on a financial interest of an official can be determined
by consulting 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18702.

     If your constituent wants more specific advice, or wants
the protection of Government Code Section 83114(b), which
provides immunity to persons who act upon advice from the
Commission, his or her identity will have to be provided.

     If you have any further questions, please feel free to
call me.

Very truly yours,

Barbara A. Miliman
Chief
Legal Division

BAM:plh

SACRAMENTO ADDRESS
STATE CAPITOL
SACRAMENTO 95814

Assembly
California Legislature

BYRON SHER
ASSEMBLYMAN, TWENTY-FIRST DISTRICT

March 3, 1981


Ms. Barbara Milman
Chief, Legal Division
Fair Political Practices Commission
1100 K Street
Sacramento, Ca  95814

Dear Ms. Milman:

     A constituent has requested that I seek your advice re-
garding the possible existence of a conflict of interest with
regard to a potential elected officeholder in a local govern-
ing body.

     The potential officeholder is a member of an Association
which engages in the practice of real estate appraisal.  The
rights and duties of members of the Association are set out in
the enclosed Articles of Association and Bill of Sale.  A member
of the Association other than the potential officeholder has as
a client the entity whose governing body the potential office-
holder would like to join.  As I understand it, there is neither
fee splitting nor profit-sharing among members of the Association.

     In particular, the constituent is concerned whether:

     1.  the potential officeholder would have to disqualify
him or her self with regard to any matters concerning clients
of other members of the Association; and

     2.  any general conflict considerations arise by virtue
of the relationship between the entity and another member
of the Association.

     I would appreciate receiving an Advice Letter from you regard-
ing these questions, and any other considerations you deem relevant.

     Thank you very much.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        [signature]
                                        Byron D. Sher
                                        Assemblyman
Enclosure                               21st District
BDS:wlc

ARTICLES OF ASSOCIATION

A, B, C and D
voluntarily associate themselves as Associates pursuant to the terms and
conditions set forth in these Articles of Association.

ARTICLE I. NATURE OF ASSOCIATION

Type of Business

1.01 The Associates shall individually engage in the practice of
real estate appraisal and the purpose of the Association shall be for the
pooling of resources and for the sharing of expenses incurred in the business
of real estate appraisal.

Name of Association


Term of Association

1.03 The Association shall commence on January 1, 19 and shall
continue until terminated under the terms of this Agreement.

ARTICLE 2. FINANCIAL

Initial Capital

2.01 Each individual Associate shall contribute to the Association
his interest in case files, statistical information, furniture, fixtures,
and equipment on the premises at the principal office of the Association or
in storage at another location. Each Associate will be considered to have
contributed an amount of a value of Twelve Thousand Five Hundred ($12,500)
Dollars.

from each Associate to be used to pay for the monthly expenses. Each

Associate will be a signatory for the account and each check drawn upon

the association account will require the signature of at least (2)

Associates. Each Associate, in turn, shall act as the business manager

and handle the detail, office and general work of running the Association

and its facilities for a six (6) month period. The first managing Associate

will be [illegible], the second managing Associate shall be [illegible]

, the third managing Associate shall be [illegible], and the

fourth managing Associate shall be [illegible]. At the completion of

the aforesaid two (2) year period, the rotation shall be repeated.

2.06 Each Associate shall have an equal voice in any decision to

expend Association funds. Any decision as to the expenditure of funds

not in the ordinary course of business shall require a majority vote of

the Associates, each Associate having one (1) vote.

2.07 No Associate shall sell, assign, mortgage, hypothecate or

encumber his interest in the Association.

ARTICLE 3. TERMINATION OF ASSOCIATION

Withdrawal of an Associate

3.01 Any Associate may voluntarily withdraw from the Association

by giving all other Associates at least sixty (60) days notice of his inten-

tion to do so.

Option to Purchase Terminated Interest

3.02 On dissolution of the Association by reason of the death of

an Associate or voluntary withdrawal of an Associate, the remaining Associates

shall have an option to purchase the interest of the deceased or withdrawing

Associate in the assets and goodwill of the Association, by paying to such

Associate or the person legally entitled thereto, the value of such interest

determined as provided in Paragraph 3.03 of these Articles.

Withdrawal of Capital

2.02 No portion of the initial contribution or subsequent contributions to the Association may be withdrawn by any Associate at any time without the express written consent of all Associates.

Books of Account

2.03 Complete and accurate accounts of all transactions of the Association shall be kept in proper books, and each of the Associates shall enter, or cause to be entered therein, a full and accurate account of all his transactions on behalf of the Association.

Inspection of Books

2.04 The books of account and other records of the Association shall, at all times, be kept in the principal place of business of the Association and each of the Associates shall, at all times, have access to, and may inspect and copy any of them.

2.05 As stated above, the primary purpose of the Association is for the sharing of expenses incurred in the operation of a real estate appraisal practice. Therefore, it is agreed among the Associates that each Associate shall bear an equal portion of the operating expenses on a monthly basis. Each month, each Associate shall be billed for a one-quarter share of the expenses incurred for the prior month. Each month each Associate shall cause to be deposited into the Association bank account his share of the operating expenses.

2.06 The Association shall establish a separate Association bank account in the name of the Association at such bank or banks in Santa Clara County, California as may from time to time be selected by a majority vote of all the Associates. The purpose of this account will be to accept deposits

2

The remaining Associates shall give notice of their exercise of such option
in the following manner:

a. If the Association is dissolved by the death of an Associate,
by serving written notice of their exercise of such option
on the personal representative of such deceased Associate
within sixty (60) days after the appointment and qualification
of such personal representative.

b. If the Association is dissolved by the voluntary withdrawal of
an Associate, by serving written notice of the exercise of such
option on the withdrawing Associate within sixty (60) days after
service on remaining Associates of the Notice of Withdrawal.

Purchase Price of Partnership Interest

3.03 On exercise of the option described in Paragraph 3.02 of these
Articles to purchase the Association interest of a deceased or withdrawing
Associate, the remaining Associates shall pay to the person legally entitled
thereto, the value of such interest determined as follows: Twelve Thousand
Five Hundred ($12,500) Dollars plus the actual cost less a reduction of ten
(10z) percent as depreciation for each year of any association purchases sub-
sequent to January 1, 19 . The aforementioned purchase price will be payable to
the deceased Associate's personal representative or the withdrawing Associate on
a term payment over a period of one (1) year at 10% interest.

BILL OF SALE AGREEMENT
Date: [illegible] 5 23 m 51

Effective on the above date X Inc. assigns, sets over and sells
the physical assets of X Inc. exclusive of existing accounts
receivables, automobiles and certain investments made by X Inc.,
and cash accounts of X Inc., to A, B, and C, each as to a
1/4 interest in the demised property and assets. It is
specifically understood that it is the intent of this agreement
that X Inc., A, B, and C will each have a 1/4 interest in the
assets so transferred under this Agreement and Bill of Sale.

This agreement and Bill of Sale is for the specific purpose
of transferring the physical assets of X Inc., to X Inc.,
A, B, and C, each as to a 1/4 interest.

The attached listing of assets transferred under this Bill
of Sale represents the agreed upon items between the
Associates and X Inc.

Signed this      day of      , 19   .

COPIER ROOM

1 - Drafting Table
2 - 2 Drawer Files

and Listings.

10 - Drawers of Sales Maps, Zoning Maps,
U.S.G.S. Maps & Miscellaneous Map Data
1 - Micro Fiche Viewer
1 - Paper Cutter
1 - Set Leased Market Data Books
1 - Set Multiple Listing Files, Misc. Punches,
Staplers, etc.


GENERAL OFFICE

6 - 4 drawer steel file cabinets
2 - Secretarial Desks & Returns
3 - Card Files - Appraisal Data
1 - Reception Seat Combination
4 - Plants

1 - Credenza - storage cabinet
2 - Wall Hangings

1 - Wall Clock & Calendar
1 - Stenorette Dictating Machine

Appraisal Workfolders, general office
data.

KITCHEN

1 - Wood Office Desk
1 - 30 Drawer Small Item Steel File

Wood Filing Cabinets (NIC)
containing appraisal reports, file data,
office supplies, etc.

1 - Electric Sweeper

PRIVATE OFFICE

1 - Office Desk
1 - Swivel Chair
3 - Office Chairs

PRIVATE OFFICE

PRIVATE OFFICE

MISCELLANEOUS

Files and Data stored in warehouse.
Any equity in leases, prepaid contracts on
office equipment.
Interest in leased Assessor's Books.

Files, punches, staplers, desk supplies, etc.

sections.question: In particular, the constituent is concerned whether: 1. the potential officeholder would have to disqualify him or her self with regard to any matters concerning clients of other members of the Association; and 2. any general conflict considerations arise by virtue of the relationship between the entity and another member of the Association.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether a potential local officeholder who is a member of a real estate appraisal association would face conflict of interest issues, specifically regarding disqualification on matters involving other association members' clients and general conflict considerations when another association member represents the governing body the officeholder seeks to join.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document is heavily redacted/corrupted with OCR errors making portions unreadable. This appears to be an incoming request letter TO the FPPC (from Assemblyman Sher requesting advice), not an FPPC response letter. The document includes attachments (Articles of Association and Bill of Sale) but no FPPC conclusion/advice is present in this extract. Marked as advice_letter because is_fppc_response field requires classification, but this is technically an incoming request for an advice letter. The FPPC's actual response is not included in this document.

embedding.summary: Assemblyman Byron Sher seeks an FPPC advice letter regarding potential conflict of interest issues for a constituent who is a prospective elected official and member of a real estate appraisal association, where another association member represents the entity whose governing body the prospective officeholder wishes to join.
